stories.title: Texas 3-1 Nebraska — Stewart HR Breaks Frahm's Near No-Hitter, Ends Husker Season (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Jordy Frahm had a no-hitter with 5 outs to go.

Then Jaycie Nichols singled. Then Katie Stewart hit a 3-run homer.

Texas 3, Nebraska 1. Nebraska is eliminated. Frahm's legendary career is over.

#HookEm #Huskers #NCAASoftball

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Katie Stewart has 28 home runs this season.

That's a new Texas softball program record.

Her 3-run blast in the 6th broke up Frahm's no-hitter and sent the Longhorns to today's semifinal vs. Tennessee.

#HookEm #NCAASoftball

facebook.long_form:
Jordy Frahm was five outs from the 20th no-hitter in Women's College World Series history.

Then the sixth inning happened.

Freshman Jaycie Nichols reached on an infield single to break up the no-hit bid. Kayden Henry followed with a single. And then Katie Stewart — who has been one of the most dangerous bats in college softball all season — turned on a Frahm pitch and launched it over the left field fence. Three-run homer. Texas 3, Nebraska 1.

The game was effectively over. The season was over. And so was one of the most extraordinary careers in college softball history.

Jordy Frahm finishes her career as a four-time NFCA first-team All-American and the 2026 USA Softball Collegiate Player of the Year. She had already homered to open the game — her 20th of the season — and she nearly authored one of the most memorable performances in WCWS history. She came within five outs.

For Texas, Stewart's blast (her 28th homer of the season — a new Texas program record) sends the Longhorns to today's noon ET semifinal against Tennessee. The defending champions are alive.

Nebraska's season — 52 wins, a first WCWS since 2013, a 27-game winning streak — ends at Devon Park. It was a season to remember. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: Five outs from a no-hitter. Then one swing ended it all. Stewart's 3-run homer eliminates Nebraska. Frahm's brilliant career ends at the WCWS. Texas is headed to the semis. 🥎 #HookEm #Huskers #NCAASoftball

article.headline: Five Outs From History: Stewart's Homer Eliminates Nebraska, Ends Frahm's Career

stories.title: Texas Tech 8-7 UCLA in 9 Innings — Canady Rebounds, Megan Grant's Season Ends (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
NiJaree Canady was struggling all game.

Then she struck out the final batter in extras to end it.

Texas Tech 8, UCLA 7 in 9 innings. Megan Grant's record-breaking season is over.

#WreckEm #WCWS #NCAASoftball

facebook.long_form:
NiJaree Canady was not at her best Sunday night. She hadn't been at her best for the entire WCWS, carrying a 7.16 ERA into the game after entering the tournament with a 1.62 season mark.

But when Texas Tech needed the final out in the ninth inning — after a wild 8-7 game that went back and forth for nine innings — Canady was the one standing on the mound. And she delivered the game-ending strikeout of Bri Alejandre.

Texas Tech wins 8-7 in nine innings, and the No. 11 seed is heading to the national semifinal.

The Red Raiders made it happen in the top of the ninth when Taylor Pannell led off with a single, moved up, and scored on Kaitlyn Terry's RBI double to left-center. Mihyia Davis scored on a UCLA error to give Tech a two-run cushion heading into the bottom of the ninth.

Canady slammed the door. No. 11 seed. National semifinal. Tonight vs. No. 1 Alabama at 7 PM on ESPN.

On the other side: Megan Grant's season is over. The most prolific home-run hitter in college softball history — 42 home runs this season, an NCAA record — exits the WCWS with UCLA. It's a bittersweet ending to a historic career. Grant hit 42 this season and 91 in her career (both records). What a player. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: Canady's game-ending strikeout. Texas Tech survives. Megan Grant's 42-HR season ends in extras. No. 11 seed in the WCWS Final Four. 🥎 #WreckEm #WCWS #NCAASoftball

stories.title: Tennessee vs. Texas WCWS Semifinal — Rematch at Noon ET on ESPN Today (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Tennessee vs. Texas. Rematch. Noon ET on ESPN.

Tennessee beat Texas 2-1 in their first WCWS meeting.

Texas has won two games since to fight back to the semis.

Pickens vs. Kavan. Everything on the line. Don't miss it. 🥎

#VolsSoftball #HookEm #NCAASoftball

facebook.long_form:
They already played once at this WCWS — and Tennessee won.

Now they're playing again, and this time the championship series is on the line.

Tennessee (2-0 at the WCWS) faces Texas in today's noon ET semifinal on ESPN. The Lady Vols beat the Longhorns 2-1 back in their opening-round meeting, sending Texas to the loser's bracket. The Longhorns have since beaten Mississippi State (Kavan's 78-pitch shutout, 600th career K) and Nebraska (Stewart's 3-run HR) to fight their way back to the Final Four.

Karlyn Pickens (6.2 IP in Tennessee's last win, outstanding all postseason) will start against Teagan Kavan (the defending champion's ace, fresh and sharp). Emma Clarke is riding tournament momentum with four home runs in this postseason run.

One of these programs is going to the championship series starting June 3. The other is going home.

Noon. ESPN. This is what the WCWS is all about. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: Tennessee vs. Texas. Rematch in the WCWS semifinals. Noon ET on ESPN. They've already played — can Tennessee win again, or does Texas get its revenge? 🥎 #VolsSoftball #HookEm #NCAASoftball

stories.title: Alabama vs. Texas Tech WCWS Semifinal — 7 PM ET ESPN Tonight (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
No. 1 Alabama vs. No. 11 Texas Tech. Tonight. 7 PM on ESPN.

Alabama (2-0 at WCWS): Briski near-flawless. The Tide look unbeatable.

Texas Tech: 9th-inning comeback last night. Canady still standing.

Cinderella meets the machine. 🥎

#RollTide #WreckEm #NCAASoftball

facebook.long_form:
No. 1 Alabama. No. 11 Texas Tech.

Nobody predicted this semifinal. And yet here we are.

Alabama has been one of the most dominant teams in this WCWS. Jocelyn Briski (25-3, SEC Pitcher of the Year) has been near-flawless, throwing her fourth one-hitter in six starts in Saturday's win over Nebraska. The Crimson Tide are 2-0 in Oklahoma City and have not trailed at any point in either game.

Texas Tech has survived everything. Consecutive elimination games. A struggling ace. A game that went to nine innings. And now they've reached the national semifinal as the No. 11 seed — the biggest Cinderella story of the 2026 WCWS.

NiJaree Canady may have struggled by her standards (7.16 WCWS ERA), but she struck out the final batter to end the UCLA game. She's still capable of dominating on any given night. Tonight, facing Alabama, she needs to find that form.

There's also the off-field backdrop: Tennessee coach Karen Weekly and Tech's Taylor Pannell (a former Volunteer) have been at the center of a viral handshake controversy since Saturday. Tonight it's a different matchup, but the Tennessee/Tech story is still part of the atmosphere in Oklahoma City.

Tonight. 7 PM. ESPN. The Cinderella run continues — or Alabama proves why they've been No. 1 all season. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: No. 1 Alabama vs. No. 11 Texas Tech. Tonight at 7 PM on ESPN. The Cinderella of the 2026 WCWS faces the tournament's most dominant team. 🥎 #RollTide #WreckEm #NCAASoftball

article.headline: No. 1 Alabama vs. No. 11 Texas Tech: The WCWS Semifinal Nobody Predicted

stories.title: The Handshake Heard Around the WCWS — Weekly vs. Pannell (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Taylor Pannell said Tennessee coach Karen Weekly told her she "made a mistake" in the WCWS handshake line Saturday.

Weekly calls it an "outright lie."

ABC showed video. The background: Pannell transferred from Tennessee to Texas Tech.

#VolsSoftball #WreckEm #NCAASoftball

facebook.long_form:
The WCWS had its biggest off-field moment of the tournament Saturday night — and it happened in the postgame handshake line.

Texas Tech third baseman Taylor Pannell — who played three seasons at Tennessee before transferring to Texas Tech last summer — said after the Red Raiders' 2-1 elimination loss to Tennessee that Lady Vols coach Karen Weekly told her in the handshake line that she "made a mistake" by leaving Tennessee.

Weekly responded directly: she called it "an outright lie," said she went just as fast past Pannell as any other player, and didn't even know where Pannell was in the line.

ABC then aired video during Sunday's Texas-Nebraska broadcast. Reporter Holly Rowe, standing near the handshake line, said she heard Weekly say "good game" to each Red Raiders player. The video itself was inconclusive.

Pannell's father, Brandon, entered the conversation on social media, posting that "Karen Weekly was right that my kid did make a mistake and that was to play for her."

The backstory adds context: Weekly publicly alleged tampering when Pannell transferred from Tennessee to Texas Tech. That accusation has been part of the narrative between these programs ever since.

The story continues tonight — the two programs' rivalry doesn't disappear just because it's Alabama in the other dugout. Tennessee plays Texas in the noon semifinal. Texas Tech faces Alabama at 7 PM. And the drama of the past 48 hours hangs over the day at Devon Park. 🥎
facebook.image_caption: She said "you made a mistake." The coach says that's a lie. The WCWS handshake controversy between Tennessee's Karen Weekly and Texas Tech's Taylor Pannell, explained. 🥎 #VolsSoftball #WreckEm #WCWS

article.headline: The WCWS Handshake Drama: Karen Weekly, Taylor Pannell, and What We Know
